items.title: Classification by age and morphology
items.contentHtml:
Classification by age and morphology

  • Senile cataract (>50 years): nuclear, cortical and posterior subcapsular.
  • Congenital cataract: present at birth or in early years.
  • Traumatic cataract: due to contusion or penetrating, it can form a rosette cataract.
  • Cataract metabolic: diabetes (snowflake cortical), galactosemia (oil drop cataract).
  • Morphological classification: nuclear (central sclerosis), cortical (peripheral wedge opacities), posterior subcapsular (granular opacity in posterior pole).
  • Grades of opacity: LOCS III (Lens Opacities Classification System III) to standardize; grades NO1-NO6 for nuclear, C1-C5 for cortical, P1-P4 for posterior subcapsular.

items.title: Types of congenital cataract
items.contentHtml:
Types of congenital cataract

  • Zonular (laminar) cataract: affects an area of ​​the lens, the most common; it is usually bilateral and symmetrical.
  • Anterior polar cataract: anterior capsular opacity, generally non-progressive, of autosomal dominant inheritance.
  • Posterior polar cataract: posterior capsular opacity, risk of capsular rupture during surgery if persistent (remnants of hyaloid artery).
  • Congenital nuclear cataract: central opacity (embryo/fetal), bilateral, it is usually dense and requires early surgery.
  • Cerulean cataract (blue dotted): small blue-gray opacities in the cortex, autosomal dominant inheritance, generally does not affect visual acuity.
  • Snowflake cataract (metabolic): associated with diabetes mellitus or galactosemia.

items.title: Posterior subcapsular cataract and steroids
items.contentHtml:
Posterior subcapsular cataract and steroids

  • Posterior subcapsular cataract (PSC): granular opacity in the posterior region of the lens, just below the capsule; It produces photophobia and poor near vision due to glare.
  • Association with steroids: chronic use of topical, systemic or inhaled corticosteroids is the main cause; dose dependent and exposure time.
  • Pathophysiology: steroids inhibit the Na+/K+-ATPase pump in the lenticular epithelium, water accumulation and protein aggregation.
  • Other risk factors: radiotherapy, trauma, diabetes, chronic uveitis.
  • Management: if possible, reduce or withdraw the corticosteroid; cataract surgery with phacoemulsification if it affects quality of life.
  • Differential: CSP due to steroids is usually bilateral and symmetrical, unlike traumatic or uveitis, which can be unilateral.

items.title: Nuclear cataract and myopia
items.contentHtml:
Nuclear cataract and myopia

  • Nuclear cataract: sclerosis of the lens nucleus, produces myopization (refractive change towards myopia) due to an increase in the refractive index.
  • Physiopathology: aggregation of crystalline proteins, increase in central optical density, generates a more lens effect convergent.
  • Clinical: progressive decrease in distance visual acuity, paradoxical improvement of near vision (second vision) in presbyopic patients.
  • Examination: yellowish opacity (nuclear sclerosis) is observed with slit lamp, grades NO1 to NO6 (LOCS III).
  • Differential diagnosis: simple myopia, keratoconus, open angle glaucoma (can coexist).
  • Surgical indication: when myopization does not adequately correct or the opacity interferes with daily activities.

items.title: Maturation and hypermature cataract
items.contentHtml:
Maturation and hypermature cataract

  • Immature cataract: partial opacity of the lens, fundus visible.
  • Mature cataract: complete opacity, grayish-white lens, fundus not visible.
  • Hypermature cataract: evolution of the mature one; liquefaction of the cortex, nucleus sinks downward (morgagnian cataract), wrinkled capsule. phacoanaphylactic.
  • Management: cataract surgery, but with greater surgical risk (fragile capsule, hard core, prolonged phacoemulsification).
  • Indication: elective surgery in mature, urgent if there is ocular hypertension or inflammation.

items.title: Indications for cataract surgery
items.contentHtml:
Indications for cataract surgery

  • Main indication: loss of visual acuity affecting daily activities (VA <20/40 or functional disability).
  • Absolute indications: mature/hypermature cataract causing phacolytic or phacomorphic glaucoma, uveitis phacoanaphylactic.
  • Relative indications: need to visualize the fundus of the eye (diabetic retinopathy, AMD), narrow-angle glaucoma due to intumescent lens, severe myopization due to nuclear cataract.
  • Contraindications: active ocular infection (conjunctivitis, blepharitis), uncontrolled intraocular inflammation, absence of light perception (if not there is another indication).
  • Preoperative evaluation: biometry (axial length, keratometry), endothelial count, fundus examination, intraocular pressure.
  • Surgical timing: individualized; in congenital cataract, early surgery (<6 weeks if bilateral, <3 months if unilateral) to avoid amblyopia.

items.title: Phacoemulsification technique
items.contentHtml:
Phacoemulsification technique

  • Phacoemulsification: standard surgical technique for cataract; uses ultrasound to fragment and aspirate the nucleus.
  • Key steps: corneal or scleral incision (2.2-2.8 mm), 5-6 mm continuous circular capsulorhexis (CCC), hydrodissection, phacoemulsification of the nucleus (divide and conquer technique, chopper, phaco-chop), cortex aspiration, capsular polishing, intraocular lens implantation (IOL) in capsular bag.
  • Anesthesia: topical (lidocaine + tetracaine drops) or peribulbar/subtenon.
  • IOL: usually foldable (hydrophobic/hydrophilic acrylic), power calculation by formula (SRK/T, Holladay, Hoffer Q).
  • Advantages: small incision, quick recovery, less induced astigmatism.
  • Intraoperative complications: posterior capsular rupture, fall of nucleus to vitreous, incision burn, corneal edema.

items.title: Postoperative complications: endophthalmitis
items.contentHtml:
Postoperative complications: endophthalmitis

  • Post-surgical endophthalmitis: severe intraocular infection, incidence 0.04-0.2% in phacoemulsification.
  • Presentation: ocular pain, decreased visual acuity, hyperemia, hypopyon, eyelid edema; It appears 24-72 hours postoperatively (acute) or weeks (chronic).
  • Most common germs: Staphylococcus epidermidis (most common), Staphylococcus aureus, streptococci, gram-negative bacilli.
  • Diagnosis: clinical + culture of aqueous and vitreous humor (Gram stain).
  • First-line treatment: intravitreal injection of vancomycin (1 mg/0.1 mL) + ceftazidime (2.25 mg/0.1 mL); vitrectomy if no improvement or initial vision < light perception.
  • Prophylaxis: povidone-iodine 5% on preoperative cornea and conjunctiva, intracameral antibiotics (cefuroxime 1 mg/0.1 mL), avoid entry of eyelid fluids.

items.title: Posterior capsular opacification (PCO)
items.contentHtml:
Posterior capsular opacification (PCO)

  • Posterior capsular opacification (PCO): most common late complication (20-40% at 5 years), also called secondary cataract.
  • Physiopathology: proliferation and migration of residual lenticular epithelial cells on the posterior capsule, with fibrous metaplasia (type cells). fibroblast) and formation of Elschnig bodies (globular cells).
  • Risk factors: hydrophilic acrylic IOL (lower risk than silicone), capsulorhexis <5.5 mm, young patients (greater cellular activity), uveitis, diabetes.
  • Clinical: progressive decrease in visual acuity, glare, glare, months to years after surgery.
  • Treatment: capsulotomy with Nd:YAG laser, typical dose 1-3 mJ, avoiding damage to the IOL or causing macular edema.
  • Complications of capsulotomy: increased intraocular pressure, anterior hyaloid rupture, macular edema, retinal detachment (risk <1%).

items.title: Cataract management in children
items.contentHtml:
Management of cataract in children

  • Surgical indication: congenital cataract that obstructs the visual axis (central opacity >3 mm) to prevent deprivation amblyopia.
  • Timing: severe bilateral cataract: surgery <6 weeks of age; unilateral: <3-4 months; ideal before 6-8 weeks.
  • Technique: phacoaspiration (without phacoemulsification), anterior and posterior capsulorrhexis, anterior vitrectomy, primary IOL implantation in children >1 year (in <1 year, aphakia with correction with contact lenses or secondary IOL is preferred).
  • Postoperative correction: contact lens or IOL with hypercorrection for near distance (due to inability to accommodate).
  • Complications: OCP (more common than in adults, requires early capsulotomy), secondary glaucoma, persistent amblyopia, strabismus.
  • Follow-up: visual rehabilitation with occlusion of the healthy eye (if unilateral), refraction control every 3-6 months.
